cards.title: Unicorn-or-bust is VC math, not your job
cards.caption: A small profitable exit only reads as failure in portfolio math. A VC needs one 100x; you need one good outcome. Borrowing their loss function is how founders talk themselves out of perfectly good wins.
cards.quote: Not every founder wants a unicorn. Sometimes a small profitable product with an exit is already a huge win.

cards.title: Validation starts when your customer gets narrower
cards.caption: Day 2 and he's already swapped guessing for interviews. The tell of real validation: the ICP shrinks — 'orgs with high key-person dependency' beats 'busy professionals.' If your customer only fits in a vague sentence, you haven't validated anything.

cards.title: The founder shouldn't be the hero of the founder story
cards.caption: Make the decision the protagonist. Heroes can't be argued with; decisions can be judged, copied, falsified — which is exactly why decision-led stories earn trust and bio-flexes don't.
cards.quote: The founder should not be the hero of every founder story. Make the decision the protagonist.

cards.title: RT self-promo with no lesson
cards.caption: Retweet of own 'documenting our journey' post. No transferable take, no story content on the card.

cards.title: Nobody cares that you 'always loved health food'
cards.caption: Most brand stories are founder bios with extra steps. Story isn't history — it's tension, resolution, product. The customer's problem is the plot; you're a supporting character at best.
cards.quote: Story isn't history it's tension, then resolution, then product.

cards.title: 448 likes for switching apps over a founder story
cards.caption: Feature parity is table stakes; the story is the switch trigger. People didn't leave Goodreads for better shelves — they left to root for a founder. A distribution lesson hiding in a book post.

cards.title: Reply compliment, no content
cards.caption: A reply saying 'I love the stories you share.' Nothing to caption — no story, no lesson.

cards.title: Affiliate promo dressed as a revenue story
cards.caption: 'Quiet wealth' framing plus a product pitch for Khan AI from an affiliate handle. Hustle-promo, not an honest founder story.

cards.title: Podcast episode ad
cards.caption: Company account promoting its co-founder's podcast appearance. Ad, no in-card lesson.

cards.title: Solid open rates, zero repeat buys — here's the leak
cards.caption: Every email chased the next sale; not one helped the customer win with what they'd already bought. Retention isn't more selling — it's making purchase #1 succeed so purchase #2 sells itself.

cards.title: The FedEx $5K blackjack story is survivorship bias
cards.caption: Everyone retells the bet that worked; the graveyard of founders who made the exact same bet has no biographer. It's a great story precisely because it's terrible advice.

cards.title: Vertical AI's moat isn't workflow. Cornered resources
cards.caption: The Bitter Lesson, founder edition: general models keep eating the workflow you wrapped. If your moat is UX on someone else's API, you're a feature on a countdown — proprietary data or distribution, or bust. The kind of essay worth a feed slot.

cards.title: Fan post about a podcast
cards.caption: 'Loved listening to you both' plus a link. No lesson, no story on the card.

cards.title: Layoff-to-consulting resilience post
cards.caption: 'Resilient reinvention' funnel post for a consulting brand. LinkedIn-fluff with a CTA, not a tactical lesson.

cards.title: Video-clip promo, hashtag-stuffed
cards.caption: 'Some fail but you keep trying' + watch-the-episode links. Generic motivation, one of several near-identical promos in this batch.

cards.title: Fun for you but not a biz
cards.caption: The brutal startup realisation: enjoying the build isn't demand. If you're the only user who would pay, you've got a hobby with a burn rate — and passion makes that harder to see, not easier.
cards.quote: Startup founder realisations: fun for you but not a biz

cards.title: Near-duplicate episode promo
cards.caption: 'Handle with care - a fragile startup!' Same show, same format as the other clip promos in this batch. No in-card lesson.

cards.title: Near-duplicate episode promo
cards.caption: 'Expectations blown by 10x' teaser with links and hashtags. No transferable content on the card itself.

cards.title: Near-duplicate episode promo
cards.caption: First-time VC fundraising teaser, watch-the-episode format. Duplicate of the same show's other cards.

cards.title: Founding is 'a high none other' — that's the trap
cards.caption: Om Malik names the addiction nobody audits: highs demand repetition. Founders who chase the feeling restart forever; the boring ones who outgrow it end up running actual companies.

cards.title: 'The universe points you' motivation post
cards.caption: 'There's never a perfect time, the universe points you towards your calling' — manifest-y motivation, wrong avatar, no mechanism.

cards.title: Near-duplicate episode promo
cards.caption: 'How to sell your startup in a shaky market?' — teaser link card, duplicate format of the same show's other posts.

cards.title: Near-duplicate episode promo
cards.caption: 'You can speak Chinese, right?' burnout episode teaser. Same promo template as the rest of the series in this batch.

cards.title: Ghostwriting service pitch
cards.caption: ALL-CAPS infographic selling ghostwriting to founders. Service ad, not an honest founder story.

cards.title: Content-farm brand-story link
cards.caption: 'Hustle, heart, and a smart niche' sock-company story from a brand-stories site. Promo framing, hustle language, no in-card mechanism.

cards.title: Interview users like a journalist: shut up
cards.caption: Journalists extract truth because they don't pitch. Founders contaminate every user interview by selling mid-question — then ship the answers they paid for. Speak less and the roadmap writes itself.

cards.title: Your first 10 users are a channel, not a milestone
cards.caption: Most solo founders stall right after the first users arrive. The unlock: ask 'who else struggles with this?' at the exact moment value lands. Timing the referral beats any growth hack you bolt on later.

cards.title: Headline-only link card
cards.caption: 'Startup founder lessons learned in 2014' — a bare Medium link. Nothing to take a position on.

cards.title: Nobody posts the version that embarrassed them
cards.caption: So everyone benchmarks their v1 against strangers' v12 and quits early. Survivorship bias isn't a stats footnote — it silently mis-sets your quality bar. The embarrassing first attempt IS the process working.
cards.quote: nobody posts about the version that embarrassed them

cards.title: Headline-only link card
cards.caption: 2013 entrepreneur-lessons link with no visible content. Summary-only, fails the take test.

cards.title: NewsTilt's post-mortem: you can't outsource conviction
cards.caption: A journalism startup whose founders, by their own post-mortem, didn't care about journalism — and users smelled it in weeks. You can outsource code and design; you can't outsource caring about the problem.

cards.title: 'Vision. Execution. Domination.' joke site
cards.caption: 'Supreme Founder — lessons for aspiring overlords.' Parody branding site, no honest lesson to extract.

cards.title: Everyone announces the round. Nobody logs the 60 nos
cards.caption: Kapwing's honest version: fundraising is terrible by design — you leak all the signal, investors leak none. It's a 2%-close sales pipeline, not a verdict on your idea. The honest write-ups surface when your reading filters for signal.

cards.title: Headline-only video link
cards.caption: Tony Hsieh lessons YouTube link with no visible substance. Nothing beyond the headline to work with.

cards.title: Answering the same question 3 times? That's a doc
cards.caption: Infra founder finally writes down the stories he keeps re-telling: staying up, passing audits, scaling without rot. Repeated 1:1 advice is unpaid documentation — the third ask is your table of contents.

cards.title: Headline-only video link
cards.caption: 'Founder's Lessons: Lisha Li, CEO of Rosebud AI' — bare YouTube link card, no extractable take.

cards.title: 'Most inspirational stories in fintech' reply
cards.caption: Context-free reply name-dropping Plaid/Column/Lead Bank. Inspiration framing, no lesson on the card.

cards.title: Selling dev tools: the hardest easy market
cards.caption: Your users are your peers, and peers expect free. Devtools founders learn distribution twice — once for the developers who love it, once for whoever actually signs the invoice. Mindscape's lessons age well.

cards.title: Founder sales is a debuggable pipeline
cards.caption: Engineers treat sales as a personality trait they weren't shipped with. It's a system: instrument it, find the drop-off, iterate — the same loop you run on code. Two B2B startups of proof, 153 HN upvotes of agreement.

cards.title: 'This. Shit. Is. Hard' — the bill nobody prices in
cards.caption: He priced the financial risk of leaving the cushy design role. The real invoice was identity: no title, no team, no applause. Runway math is easy — status withdrawal is the part that actually breaks people.

cards.title: The 'have-not' region has Canada's most confident founders
cards.caption: 3,000-founder survey: Atlantic Canada — written off for decades — posts Canada's highest founder confidence. Nobody's there for the scene, so everyone's there for the business. My reader pulled this off Bluesky.

cards.title: Board chair founded Acclaim. Turok stories twice a week
cards.caption: A founder's board chair was Acclaim's founder — Turok stories on loop, twice a week. Ex-founders on boards govern from the highlight reel: you don't get oversight, you get reruns. Pick advisors who are still curious.
cards.quote: I had to hear about Turok once or twice a week

cards.title: He invited the VC in. The VC decided he should go
cards.caption: Founder invites a VC in; the VC decides it's time for the founder to leave. Taking the money means hiring a boss whose math beats your equity — board control compounds faster than founder shares.
cards.quote: You can fight gravity but cannot beat it

cards.title: Failure postmortems: the only startup content with no spin
cards.caption: 'Why thinkpanda.com failed' — an autopsy, not a victory lap. Postmortems are the one startup genre where the incentive to lie is gone; ten of these teach more than a hundred launch threads. Found in my Threadverse queue.

cards.title: Product Hunt gives you a day, not a channel
cards.caption: Buried in a Show HN: after the Product Hunt spike, visibility flatlines. Launches are events; distribution is a system — a list of launch sites is a calendar, not a channel. My feed reader flagged this one.

cards.title: 3 years of nights and weekends. $0. Now what?
cards.caption: Ask HN: three years of nights-and-weekends startups, zero dollars, and now every idea on the list feels radioactive. The burnout isn't from the work — it's from shipping into silence. Traction problems wear motivation-problem costumes.

cards.title: Four products to learn one boring lesson
cards.caption: 'Demand comes first' costs nothing to read and four dead products to believe. Builders don't skip validation out of ignorance — building feels like progress, asking feels like rejection. The lesson is emotional, not informational.
cards.quote: I built 4 products before learning the boring SaaS lesson: demand comes first

cards.title: Indie revenue screenshots are marketing, not data
cards.caption: A builder pulled indie revenue from real signals instead of self-reported screenshots — estimates labeled. Build-in-public MRR posts are ads in a candor costume; they select for storytellers, not operators. Came up in my curated feed.

cards.title: A 13-year-old just found the 'one more feature' trap
cards.caption: 13-year-old dev, two micro-SaaS: 'I kept telling myself one more feature would bring traction.' Feature work is how builders procrastinate on distribution — the trap doesn't care if you're 13 or 33.

cards.title: Tagline fix: name the user's verb, not your category
cards.caption: Before: 'Marketing database for startups.' After: 'Explore the best places to promote your startup.' Categories describe you; verbs describe them. Users don't buy what a thing is — they buy what they get to do next.

cards.title: Why YC gives away its best startup ideas for free
cards.caption: YC publishes 'Requests for Startups' — real problems, real demand, free. They can afford to: ideas are worthless without execution, and every builder who bites becomes their dealflow. Use the list; know whose funnel you're in.

cards.title: He gave up on his SaaS — and sold the boilerplate
cards.caption: Dev rage-quit building weekend apps over billing and auth glue, then shipped the boilerplate as the product. Half of indie hacking is this arc: the tooling detour outearns the destination. Sell the shovel you built for yourself.

cards.title: Outsiders get rich on startups devs call 'too hard'
cards.caption: The indie bubble optimizes for easy-build SaaS; outsiders blunder into marketplaces, ecommerce, hardware — and stumble onto $$$. Knowing what's 'too hard' is how a community fences itself out of markets. Naivety is an edge.

cards.title: We archive wins. Failure data just evaporates
cards.caption: A builder wants a public database of dead startups: name, cause of death, founder. Survivorship bias isn't a bug in startup lore — it's the genre. The most informative dataset in tech is the one nobody bothers to keep.

cards.title: MVPs ship in hours now. The moat moved to distribution
cards.caption: Ask HN: vibe coding collapsed build time from months to hours — so shipping fast differentiates nothing. When execution is commoditized, distribution is the scarce skill, and most builders are still optimizing the solved half.

cards.title: The unicorn-or-dead binary is fund math, not your P&L
cards.caption: 'Your startup doesn't need to be a unicorn' — the missing middle keeps control and profits. VCs need outliers because portfolio math demands it; you don't. A $2M/yr business is a failure to a fund and a fortune to a founder.
